1.synthesis: Proverbs 23:1–28 continues the Thirty Sayings of the Wise collection that began in Proverbs 22:17, presenting a series of extended admonitions on appetite, wealth, parental honor, adultery, and the fear of YHWH. Unlike the terse antithetical proverbs of chapters 10–22, these sayings often extend to three or four verses with detailed instruction and motivating reason-clauses. The opening cluster addresses dining etiquette and the self-deception of wealth: “Do not toil to acquire wealth; be discerning enough to desist. When your eyes light on it, it is gone, for suddenly it sprouts wings, flying like an eagle toward heaven” (vv. 4–5). The miser’s table as deceptive (vv. 6–8) and the foolishness of speaking wisdom to fools (v. 9) lead into the chapter’s most urgent instruction: the command to buy truth and not sell it, and to give one’s heart to wisdom (vv. 23–26). The chapter’s emotional climax is the father’s appeal to his son: “My son, if your heart is wise, my heart too will be glad. My inmost being will exult when your lips speak what is right” (vv. 15–16). The section closes with the warning against the adulterous woman, whose house is the way to Sheol (vv. 27–28).
1.historical_context: The collection found in Proverbs 22:17–24:22 belongs to what scholars identify as a distinct wisdom corpus with notable parallels to the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope, a thirty-chapter wisdom text from approximately the thirteenth to eleventh centuries BC. Dining etiquette at a ruler’s table (vv. 1–3) was a major concern in both Egyptian court manuals and Mesopotamian wisdom literature: how one ate before patrons communicated character, social awareness, and self-control. The miser (’ayin-tov, one with an evil eye, v. 6) was a recognized social type whose external hospitality concealed internal stinginess. The “ancient landmark” protection of orphans (v. 10) connects to the legal provision for the fatherless throughout the Mosaic code (Deuteronomy 10:18; Psalm 68:5). The warning against the adulteress as a deep pit (v. 27) uses the language of trap-hunting, which appears in wisdom literature from Mesopotamia to Egypt as an image for the woman who destroys without apparent violence.
1.literary_structure: Proverbs 23:1–28 is structured in loose thematic clusters typical of the Thirty Sayings genre: appetite and wealth (vv. 1–8); the instruction of fools and the fatherless (vv. 9–11); discipline and parental joy (vv. 12–16); the fear of YHWH and its future reward (vv. 17–21); the command to buy truth and honor parents (vv. 22–26); and the adulterous woman as pit and predator (vv. 27–28). The genre shifts between direct commands (second-person address), motivation clauses, and illustrative vignettes. The father-to-son format of the prologue is fully revived: the emotional register of “my son, give me your heart” (v. 26) is warmer and more personal than the anonymous sentence proverbs of chapters 10–22.
1.theological_application: “Do not let your heart envy sinners, but continue in the fear of the LORD all the day. Surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off” (vv. 17–18) is the section’s theological anchor: the fear of YHWH is commended not merely as a moral attitude but as the ground of future hope. The person who envies sinners has lost sight of the long-term account; the person who fears YHWH daily is investing in a future that has no end. “Buy truth, and do not sell it; buy wisdom, instruction, and understanding” (v. 23) uses market-transaction language to make the point that wisdom is worth its price: it cannot be merely borrowed or inherited but must be personally acquired at cost. Parental discipline commanded in vv. 13–14 (“if you strike him with the rod, he will not die; if you strike him with the rod, you will save his soul from Sheol”) frames correction in life-and-death terms, grounding it in genuine parental love rather than harshness.
1.christology: “Buy truth, and do not sell it” (v. 23) names the acquisition-at-cost of wisdom whose ultimate object is Christ, who is “the way, and the truth, and the life” (John 14:6). To possess truth is not to hold a proposition but to be in relationship with a person; to sell it is to abandon that person for something cheaper. The future and hope of v. 18 (“your hope will not be cut off”) is the Old Testament form of the hope that the resurrection makes explicit: those who fear YHWH have a future beyond what the present circumstances suggest. The adulteress whose house is the way to Sheol (v. 27) is the countertype to Wisdom whose house leads to life (Proverbs 9:1–6); Christ, as Wisdom incarnate, is the invitation to the feast that leads to life rather than the pit that ends in Sheol.

29.synthesis: Proverbs 23:29–35 is one of the most vivid extended poems in the wisdom collection: a dramatic portrait of the drunkard that begins with a riddle, develops into social observation, offers an almost seductive description of wine’s initial pleasures, and ends with the grim punchline of addiction. “Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has strife? Who has complaining? Who has wounds without cause? Who has redness of eyes? Those who tarry long over wine; those who go to try mixed wine” (vv. 29–30). The accumulation of woes in the riddle-opening pulls the reader into the social panorama of intoxication’s casualties before naming its cause. The poem then pivots to describe wine’s appeal with notable ambivalence: “Do not look at wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup and goes down smoothly” (v. 31). The appeal is real; the wisdom tradition does not pretend it is not. But the closing verses reveal the cost: snakebite, strange visions, the staggering sailor, the man who is struck but feels nothing and wakes only to seek another drink (vv. 34–35).
29.historical_context: Wine (yayin) and mixed wine (mesekh) were staple commodities in ancient Israel, consumed at feasts, used in worship, and stored as household wealth. The warning against excessive consumption belongs to a broader ANE wisdom tradition: Egyptian and Mesopotamian texts similarly warned against drunkenness as a destroyer of social standing and self-control. The Nazarite vow (Numbers 6:3) and the prohibition of wine for priests on duty (Leviticus 10:9) represent contexts of special abstinence within a culture that accepted moderate consumption. The “redness of eyes” (chashkas ’ênayyim) and the vivid physical symptoms of intoxication (wounds without cause, the staggering sailor) reflect accurate physiological observation. The closing verse — “When shall I awake? I must have another drink” (v. 35) — is wisdom’s most acute description of addiction: the self-reinforcing craving that sleep and injury do not interrupt.
29.literary_structure: The drunkenness poem of vv. 29–35 is formally distinctive within Proverbs: it opens with a riddle-series of seven unanswered questions (vv. 29–30), then delivers the answer (those who linger over wine), issues a command with motivation (vv. 31–32), and closes with a vivid extended analogy and the drunkard’s self-revealing speech (vv. 33–35). The shift from prohibition to analogy to self-quotation gives the poem a narrative momentum rare in the wisdom literature. The serpent-bite image (v. 32, “in the end it bites like a serpent and stings like an adder”) connects to the Eden narrative where the serpent’s appeal was similarly sensory and the consequences delayed.
29.theological_application: The drunkard poem exposes the structure of temptation: initial appeal (wine sparkles, goes down smoothly, v. 31), delayed consequence (serpent bite, v. 32), and self-reinforcing bondage (“I must have another drink,” v. 35). This is wisdom’s phenomenological account of how enslavement to appetite works: not through violent compulsion but through incremental surrender of self-governance. The New Testament command to “not get drunk with wine… but be filled with the Spirit” (Ephesians 5:18) uses the same category of displacement: the question is not whether something fills and governs, but what that something is. Communities shaped by Proverbs 23 should be honest about the genuine appeal of what destroys and equip their members to see the end-consequence before experiencing it.
29.christology: The bondage to addiction described in v. 35 (“When shall I awake? I must have another drink”) is one form of the slavery to sin from which Christ sets free: “Everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin. The slave does not remain in the house forever; the son remains forever. So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed” (John 8:34–36). The serpent-bite image (v. 32) evokes the serpent of Eden whose bite brought death; Christ is lifted up as the bronze serpent of Numbers 21:9 was lifted up (John 3:14–15), so that those bitten by sin might look to him and live. The wisdom that names the appeal of destruction honestly before commending an alternative is the wisdom embodied in a Savior who entered the world of human temptation and overcame it from the inside.
